title: Wasserstein-2 gradient flows and the geometry of entropy production in classical and quantum stochastic thermodynamics
authors: Olga Movilla Miangolarra, Ralph Sabbagh, Artemy Kolchinsky
abstract: The second law does more than set the direction of thermodynamic evolution: it endows nonequilibrium transformations with an underlying geometry. In this work, we provide a unified geometric description of entropy production in classical and quantum thermodynamics based on Wasserstein-2 structures arising from gradient flows of free energy. We review how relaxation to equilibrium, in overdamped diffusions, discrete detailed-balanced Markov chains, and dissipative Lindblad dynamics, can be formulated as a gradient flow on the space of states. The associated Wasserstein-2 distance bounds entropy production, yielding a finite-time refinement of the second law. We extend this framework beyond purely dissipative dynamics by introducing generalized Wasserstein-2 metrics that incorporate conservative (Hamiltonian) dynamics in both classical inertial systems and open quantum systems, yielding intrinsic distances that exactly characterize minimal entropy production under fixed dissipative mobilities. We establish equivalence bounds between purely dissipative and Hamiltonian-dissipative geometries, explicitly quantifying how inertial or coherent dynamics can reduce dissipation. Finally, when restricted to equilibrium distributions, we recover the thermodynamic length of linear response-including the quantum thermodynamic length-thereby linking optimal transport, thermodynamic length, and counterdiabatic protocols within a single geometric framework. All in all, our results extend the Riemannian program of thermodynamics further from equilibrium and provide a geometric foundation for optimal protocols beyond the overdamped setting.
summary: This paper unifies the geometric understanding of entropy production in classical and quantum thermodynamics using Wasserstein-2 metrics derived from free energy gradient flows. It shows that these metrics provide finite-time refinements to the second law and rigorously connects optimal transport theory to fundamental thermodynamic quantities like thermodynamic length. The framework extends beyond simple dissipation to incorporate coherent (Hamiltonian) dynamics.
main_problem: To provide a unified geometric description of entropy production in classical and quantum stochastic thermodynamics, extending the second law beyond purely dissipative dynamics.
main_result: The framework establishes that the Wasserstein-2 distance bounds entropy production, offering a finite-time refinement of the second law, and connects optimal transport to thermodynamic length.
method: The authors utilize Wasserstein-2 structures arising from gradient flows of free energy, formulating relaxation dynamics for continuous, discrete, and quantum systems.
model_or_system: The work covers classical overdamped diffusions, discrete Markov chains, and open quantum systems evolving under Lindblad dynamics. The geometry is defined on the space of states (probability distributions or density matrices).
key_observables: Entropy production, Free energy functional, Wasserstein-2 distance, Thermodynamic length.
important_parameters: Dissipative mobilities, Hamiltonian coupling strength ($\gamma$), Time ($	au$), Temperature ($ eta$).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes the existence of a smooth manifold of states and relies on establishing equivalence bounds between different geometric formulations (e.g., Hamiltonian-dissipative vs. purely dissipative).
figures_summary: Figure 1 summarizes the Wasserstein-2 geometric picture for purely dissipative and mixed Hamiltonian-dissipative systems. Figure 2 illustrates the gradient flow of the free energy functional on the state space.
paper_structure: The paper systematically reviews the gradient flow formulation across continuous, discrete, and quantum settings, deriving the associated Wasserstein-2 metrics. It then establishes bounds relating entropy production to these geometric distances, culminating in the recovery of thermodynamic length at equilibrium.
why_interesting: This work provides a powerful geometric lens (optimal transport) to analyze non-equilibrium processes in quantum and classical systems, offering quantitative bounds on irreversible processes beyond the standard second law.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Summary of the Wasserstein-2 geometric picture of stochastic and quantum thermodynamics in purely dissipative (left) and mixed Hamiltonian-dissipative (right) systems. The Wasserstein-2 geometry arises from a gradient flow structure in purely dissipative systems (continuous, discrete, and quantum), and from a combination of gradient and Hamiltonian flows in mixed systems (continuous and quantum). In all cases, the distance squared bounds entropy production in finite-time transitions.
FIG. 2. The state x evolves on the Riemannian manifold (M, g) along the gradient flow of the free energy F.

FIG. 3. (a) Entropy produced with the constant Hamilto- nian, ∫ τ 0 ˙Σdt, and with the optimal quadratic Hamiltonian, W G 2,h(ρ0, ργ τ)2/τ, between endpoints ρ0, ργ τ , as a function of the friction coefficient γ = RL = 1/RC. The shaded area il- lustrates the upper and lower bounds in terms of the purely dissipative metric. Constants τ, T, C, and L are set to 1. (b) RLC circuit with resistors at temperature T.

FIG. 4. Minimum entropy counterdiabatic switching for gene regulation. (a) Illustration of the discrete Markov process, the operator site is depicted in green, the repressor in blue, and the corepressor in red. Portrayal of (b) optimal trajectories, (c) optimal potentials, (d) resulting entropy production rate, and (e) detailed-balanced counterdiabatic rates that realize the optimal trajectory in finite time. We have set τ = 5 and T = 1.

FIG. 5. Comparison between entropy produced and the de- rived bounds for different values of the decay rate γ; β, ω, τ are set to 1. (a) The initial state is not diagonal, and the Hamiltonian term plays a role. (b) The initial state is diago- nal, and the Hamiltonian term plays no role in the dynamics.
